unit.name: Classical recognition, AI winter, and the cognitive science north star
unit.description: Reviews the classical computer vision era, the limitations that led to AI winter, and the cognitive/neuroscience findings that clarified what strong visual recognition should look like.
unit.summary: Li surveys 1970s to 1990s classical approaches such as generalized cylinders, compositional models, and edge detection, noting that these methods made progress but often produced limited representations like sketches and edges [ts=1041s] [ts=1135s]. She explains that this gap between ambition and results contributed to AI winter, when enthusiasm and funding declined because systems did not deliver on their promises [ts=1143s] [ts=1179s]. The lecture then pivots to cognitive science and neuroscience as a North Star for the field, highlighting studies showing that recognition depends on scene context, not only local object pixels [ts=1197s] [ts=1238s]. Additional experiments demonstrate how remarkably fast human visual recognition is, including RSVP-style detection at 100 ms per frame and EEG evidence that categorization signals emerge around 150 ms after image onset [ts=1260s] [ts=1378s]. Finally, discoveries of specialized brain areas for faces, places, and body parts reinforce the idea that recognition is central and highly structured in biological vision [ts=1382s] [ts=1434s].

key_points.text: Classical vision methods included recognition via parts and edge detection, but their outputs often remained shallow.
AI winter is attributed to dwindling enthusiasm and funding after systems like expert systems and classical vision failed to deliver.
Scene context affects object recognition; seeing the whole world changes how targets are perceived.
Human vision can detect targets in rapidly shown frames at roughly 100 ms exposure per frame.
EEG evidence suggests category-specific signals emerge around 150 ms after viewing a natural scene.
Humans have specialized neural areas for recognizing faces, places, and body parts.

kp_catalog.name: Classical computer vision and hand-crafted features
kp_catalog.description: Pre-neural-network image pipelines that replaced raw pixels with human-designed descriptors such as color histograms or HOG before classification. The pre-deep-learning era emphasized parts, edges, grouping, matching, and handcrafted representations, with limited success on full recognition.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because classical computer vision and hand-crafted features clarifies pre-neural-network image pipelines that replaced raw pixels with human-designed descriptors such as color histograms or HOG before classification, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices while roles split between optional and supporting and importance labels split between high and medium.

kp_catalog.name: Human recognition is fast, contextual, and specialized
kp_catalog.description: Cognitive and neuroscience studies show that recognition depends on scene context, happens rapidly, and recruits specialized processing areas.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because human recognition is fast, contextual, and specialized clarifies cognitive and neuroscience studies show that recognition depends on scene context, happens rapidly, and recruits specialized processing areas, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Deep learning resurgence from data, compute, and scale
kp_catalog.description: Why modern neural NLP depends on a set of training and modeling ideas that made deep networks workable after years of difficulty. Large-scale datasets, GPU computation, and neural architectures converged to unlock modern deep learning for vision.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because deep learning resurgence from data, compute, and scale formalizes how why modern neural NLP depends on a set of training and modeling ideas that made deep networks workable after years of difficulty, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
